LITERATURE CARD
PhD Dissertation Literature Review
1. Bibliographic Metadata
Full Citation (APA 7): Ting, D. S. W., Cheung, C. Y.-L., Lim, G., Tan, G. S. W., Quang, N. D., Gan, A., Hamzah, H., Garcia-Franco, R., San Yeo, I. Y., Lee, S. Y., Wong, E. Y. M., Sabanayagam, C., Baskaran, M., Ibrahim, F., Tan, N. C., Finkelstein, E. A., Lamoureux, E. L., Wong, I. Y., Bressler, N. M., ... Wong, T. Y. (2017). Development and validation of a deep learning system for diabetic retinopathy and related eye diseases using retinal images from multiethnic populations with diabetes. JAMA, 318(22), 2211–2223.
DOI: 10.1001/jama.2017.18152
Journal: JAMA (Journal of the American Medical Association)
Year: 2017
Publication Type: Empirical – Clinical validation study
Research Domain: Deep learning for diabetic retinopathy screening; multiethnic clinical validation; multi-disease detection (DR, glaucoma, AMD)
2. Study Type Classification
•	External validation study – validated on 10 external multiethnic datasets from different countries, cameras, and clinical settings
•	Cross-dataset validation – DLS trained on SIDRP 2010–2013 and evaluated on temporally distinct SIDRP 2014–2015 plus 10 independent cohorts
•	Clinical prospective-equivalent validation – primary validation on an ongoing national screening program dataset (SIDRP 2014–2015)
•	CNN-based classification study – convolutional neural network architecture (details in supplement)
Justification: The study combines internal temporal validation with extensive external validation across multiethnic, multi-camera, multi-setting cohorts, making it one of the most comprehensively validated DR deep learning studies at its time of publication.
3. Research Problem
The study addresses whether a deep learning system can achieve clinically acceptable performance in detecting referable diabetic retinopathy, vision-threatening diabetic retinopathy, referable possible glaucoma, and referable age-related macular degeneration from retinal images obtained in real-world, multiethnic clinical populations.
Related to: 
•	Generalization – primary focus on whether DLS trained on one population generalizes across 10 multiethnic, multi-camera cohorts
•	Clinical deployment – evaluates fully automated and semiautomated screening models
•	Multi-disease detection – extends beyond DR to glaucoma and AMD
Not directly focused on preprocessing, architecture scaling, or lesion detection.
4. Datasets Used
Training Dataset
Name: SIDRP 2010–2013 (Singapore National Diabetic Retinopathy Screening Program)
Public/Private: Private (national screening program)
Sample Size: 76,370 images (38,185 eyes; 13,099 patients) for DR; 125,189 images for glaucoma; 72,610 images for AMD
Class Taxonomy: Referable DR (binary: moderate NPDR or worse + DME + ungradable vs. non-referable); Vision-threatening DR (binary: severe NPDR or worse vs. rest)
Train/Val/Test Split: Training on SIDRP 2010–2013; primary validation on temporally separate SIDRP 2014–2015
Primary Validation Dataset
Name: SIDRP 2014–2015
Public/Private: Private
Sample Size: 71,896 images (35,948 eyes; 14,880 patients)
Reference Standard: Retinal specialist (≥5 years experience), masked to DLS output
10 External Validation Datasets
Total: 40,752 images across 10 cohorts from multiple countries:
Dataset	N Images	Ethnicity	Setting	Camera
Guangdong (China)	15,798	Chinese	Community	FundusVue
Singapore Malay Eye Study	3,052	Malay	Population	Canon
Singapore Indian Eye Study	4,512	Indian	Population	Canon
Singapore Chinese Eye Study	1,936	Chinese	Population	Canon
Beijing Eye Study	1,052	Chinese	Population	Canon
African American Eye Disease Study	1,968	African American	Population	Topcon
Royal Victoria Eye and Ear Hospital (Australia)	2,302	White	Clinic	Topcon
Mexican Study	1,172	Hispanic	Clinic	Topcon
Chinese Univ. of Hong Kong	1,254	Chinese	Clinic	Topcon
Univ. of Hong Kong	7,706	Chinese	Clinic	Carl Zeiss

External dataset used? Yes
Cross-dataset testing performed? Yes – 10 independent external cohorts
5. Preprocessing Pipeline
Resizing: [NOT REPORTED]
Cropping: [NOT REPORTED]
Normalization: [NOT REPORTED]
CLAHE: [NOT REPORTED]
Color Normalization: [NOT REPORTED]
Augmentation: [NOT REPORTED]
Image Quality Filtering: Ungradable images (>1/3 photograph obscured) were classified as referable. Subsidiary analysis conducted on subset with no media opacity (n = 35,055 eyes).
Lesion Enhancement: [NOT REPORTED]
Note: The article does not describe any preprocessing pipeline. Technical details are stated to be in eFigure 1 in the Supplement, which is not included in the main text.
6. Model Architecture
Architecture Type: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) – specific architecture not named in main text; described as available in eFigure 1 in the Supplement
Pretraining Source: [NOT REPORTED in main text]
Transfer Learning Protocol: [NOT REPORTED in main text]
Input Resolution: [NOT REPORTED] – images captured at 5–7 megapixels (JPEG); Hispanic cohort <1 megapixel
Loss Function: [NOT REPORTED]
Optimizer: [NOT REPORTED]
Epochs: [NOT REPORTED]
Hyperparameters: Sensitivity threshold preset at 90% for operating point selection
Training completed May 2016; validation completed May 2017.
7. Validation Design
•	Internal validation: Yes – temporally separate SIDRP 2014–2015 (trained on 2010–2013, validated on 2014–2015)
•	Cross-validation: No – single train/validation split
•	External validation: Yes – 10 independent multiethnic cohorts from different countries, cameras, and settings
•	Prospective validation: No – retrospective analysis of prospectively collected screening data
•	Multi-center validation: Yes – datasets from Singapore, China, Australia, USA, Mexico, Hong Kong
Subsidiary analyses: (1) exclusion of 6,291 overlapping patients; (2) high-quality image subset; (3) AUC stratified by age, sex, HbA1c; (4) concordance analysis with human graders.
8. Performance Metrics
Primary Validation (SIDRP 2014–2015, n = 71,896 images)
Referable Diabetic Retinopathy
Metric	DLS	Trained Professional Graders	P Value
AUC (95% CI)	0.936 (0.925–0.943)	—	—
Sensitivity (95% CI)	90.5% (87.3–93.0%)	91.2% (88.0–93.6%)	.68
Specificity (95% CI)	91.6% (91.0–92.2%)	99.3% (99.2–99.4%)	<.001

Vision-Threatening DR

Sensitivity (95% CI)	100% (94.1–100.0%)	88.5% (75.3–95.1%)	<.001
Specificity (95% CI)	91.1% (90.7–91.4%)	99.6% (99.6–99.7%)	<.001

Subgroup Analysis (Primary Validation)
Age <60y AUC: 0.980 (95% CI, 0.975–0.984)
Age ≥60y AUC: 0.920 (95% CI, 0.899–0.940)
Men AUC: 0.952 (95% CI, 0.925–0.963)
Women AUC: 0.948 (95% CI, 0.933–0.956)

HbA1c ≥8% AUC: 0.954 (95% CI, 0.942–0.964)
High-quality images AUC: 0.968–0.973
Screening Model Performance
Fully automated model: Sensitivity 93.0% (95% CI, 91.5–94.3%); Specificity 77.5% (95% CI, 77.0–77.9%)
Semiautomated model: Sensitivity 91.3% (95% CI, 89.7–92.8%); Specificity 99.5% (95% CI, 99.5–99.6%)
Statistical Tests: McNemar test for DLS vs. graders; cluster-bootstrap biased-corrected 95% CIs; exact Clopper-Pearson CI for boundary estimates; Stata version 14
Cohen’s Kappa: [NOT REPORTED]
Confusion Matrix: [NOT REPORTED as formal matrix; concordance/discordance counts provided in Table 5]
F1 Score: [NOT REPORTED]
Accuracy: [NOT REPORTED]
9. Authors’ Claims
Performance Claims
•	DLS sensitivity for referable DR (90.5%) was comparable to trained professional graders (91.2%; P = .68)
•	DLS had 100% sensitivity for vision-threatening DR, significantly higher than graders (88.5%; P < .001)
•	DLS showed clinically acceptable AUCs (>0.90) across different camera types (FundusVue, Canon, Topcon, Carl Zeiss)
Generalization Claims
•	DLS maintained clinically acceptable performance (sensitivity ≥90%) across multiethnic populations of different communities, clinics, and settings
•	AUC ranged 0.889–0.983 across 10 external validation datasets
•	Consistent performance across age, sex, and glycemic control subgroups
Clinical Applicability Claims
•	DLS could be deployed in fully automated model for communities without existing screening programs
•	Semiautomated model (DLS + human review of positives) achieves 99.5% specificity
Superiority Claims
•	No direct superiority claim over previous DLS studies; authors note their approach uses substantially larger and more diverse training/validation data compared to Gulshan et al. (2016)
10. Empirical Support Assessment
•	Generalization claims are well-supported: 10 external datasets across 6 countries, multiple ethnicities, camera types, and grading reference standards all show AUC > 0.889
•	External validation is robust: largest multiethnic external validation of a DR DLS at time of publication; however, all datasets are retrospective
•	Confidence intervals are reported for all primary metrics (AUC, sensitivity, specificity) using cluster-bootstrap method accounting for eye-level clustering within patients
•	Dataset size is adequate: 76,370 training images; 71,896 primary validation images; 40,752 external validation images
•	Class imbalance is present (referable DR prevalence 3.0% in primary validation, 15.4% in external sets) but not explicitly addressed through resampling or weighted loss; operating point was pre-selected at 90% sensitivity threshold
•	Statistical testing is adequate: McNemar test for paired comparisons; bootstrap CIs adjusted for clustering; P < .05 two-sided
11. Internal Validity
Overfitting Risk: Moderate-low. Temporal separation between training (2010–2013) and validation (2014–2015) mitigates data leakage. However, 6,291 patients overlap between training and primary validation sets; subsidiary analysis excluding these patients showed similar results.
Dataset Leakage Risk: Addressed. Authors explicitly identified and re-analyzed after excluding 6,291 overlapping patients. Results were consistent.
Confounders: Varying reference standards across external datasets (retinal specialists, general ophthalmologists, trained graders, optometrists) introduce heterogeneity in ground truth quality.
Augmentation Inflation Risk: Not applicable – no augmentation is reported.
Metric Reliability: High. AUC with bootstrap CIs, McNemar test for sensitivity/specificity comparisons. Appropriate statistical methodology.
12. External Validity
Cross-Population Transferability: Strong. Validated on Chinese, Malay, Indian, African American, White (Australian), Hispanic, and Hong Kong Chinese populations.
Dataset Portability: Moderate. DLS showed AUCs > 0.90 for most datasets, but lower for Singapore Malay (0.889) and Singapore Indian (0.917) with notably lower specificity (73.3–82.0%).
Clinical Feasibility: High. Study evaluated both fully automated and semiautomated deployment models. Validated within an ongoing national screening program.
Hardware Constraints: [NOT REPORTED]
13. Strengths
•	Largest and most ethnically diverse training and validation dataset for DR DLS at time of publication (~494,661 total images)
•	Temporal separation of training and validation cohorts within the same screening program
•	10 independent external validation datasets spanning 6 countries, multiple camera types, and diverse grading standards
•	Multi-disease detection (DR, glaucoma, AMD) from a single system
•	Rigorous statistical methodology with cluster-bootstrap CIs accounting for within-patient correlation
•	Direct comparison with professional human graders using retinal specialist as reference standard
•	Evaluation of practical deployment models (fully automated vs. semiautomated)
•	Subgroup analysis by age, sex, and glycemic control
14. Limitations
Explicit (Stated by Authors)
•	Training set reference standards were not entirely based on retinal specialist grading; external datasets used varying reference standards
•	DLS black-box nature – uses multiple levels of representation without showing actual lesions, potentially affecting physician acceptance
•	DME identification from fundus photographs may miss cases requiring OCT confirmation
Implicit (Methodological)
•	Preprocessing pipeline is not described in the main text, limiting reproducibility
•	CNN architecture is not specified in the main text (only in supplement eFigure 1)
•	No lesion-level evaluation – classification is image-level/eye-level only
•	No cross-validation – single train/test split for primary analysis
•	Retrospective design – not a true prospective clinical trial
•	Class imbalance not explicitly addressed (3.0% referable DR in primary validation)
•	Specificity of DLS significantly lower than human graders for referable DR (91.6% vs. 99.3%; P < .001) and vision-threatening DR (91.1% vs. 99.6%; P < .001), raising concerns about false positive burden in screening
•	No comparison with other published DLS architectures (e.g., Gulshan et al., 2016)
15. Relevance to My Dissertation

Position in paradigm space (v5.3): P1 (end-to-end CNN; preprocessing as auxiliary step). Grounds (per SIR-9): the paper does not report preprocessing in the main text and frames the deep learning system primarily through architecture, training data scale, and the breadth of multi-ethnic external validation. Per CFC-2.9 / SIR-1, no theoretical "preprocessing is unimportant" claim is attributed to the authors; the paradigmatic attribution is grounded in the practice of deferring preprocessing details out of the main text.

Relevance to Preprocessing Dominance Hypothesis: Moderate-low. The article does not report preprocessing details, which paradoxically supports the argument that preprocessing details are often underreported in high-impact studies. Cannot be used to directly test preprocessing impact.
Relevance to Cross-Database Validation: Very high. This is a primary exemplar of rigorous cross-database validation with 10 external datasets spanning diverse populations, cameras, and settings. Directly relevant to dissertation arguments about the necessity of external validation.
Relevance to EyePACS/Messidor Benchmarking: Low. This study uses private SIDRP datasets, not public benchmarks (EyePACS, Messidor). Referenced as comparison to Gulshan et al. (2016) which used EyePACS.
Relevance to Vision Transformer Comparison: None. Pre-dates Vision Transformer era (2017). Establishes CNN baseline against which Transformer-based approaches can be compared.
Risk of Contradiction: Low. The study’s strong external validation performance could be argued as evidence that preprocessing is not critical if the DLS is sufficiently trained on large, diverse data. However, the lack of preprocessing reporting makes this inference speculative.
16. Citation-Ready Statements
1. Ting et al. (2017) demonstrated that a DLS achieved an AUC of 0.936 (95% CI, 0.925–0.943) for referable DR with sensitivity of 90.5% and specificity of 91.6% on 71,896 images from an ongoing national screening program (p. 2211).
2. External validation across 10 multiethnic cohorts (n = 40,752 images) showed AUCs ranging from 0.889 to 0.983 for referable DR, demonstrating consistent performance across Chinese, Malay, Indian, African American, White, and Hispanic populations (p. 2211–2212).
3. The DLS achieved 100% sensitivity (95% CI, 94.1–100.0%) for vision-threatening DR, significantly outperforming trained professional graders at 88.5% (P < .001), though with lower specificity (91.1% vs. 99.6%; P < .001) (Table 4, p. 2218).
4. A semiautomated screening model combining DLS with human review of positive cases achieved sensitivity of 91.3% and specificity of 99.5%, suggesting a viable deployment strategy for existing screening programs (p. 2221).
5. The study validated on ~494,661 total images from multiethnic populations, representing the largest and most diverse evaluation of a DR deep learning system at its time of publication (p. 2212).
6. DLS performance was consistent across subgroups stratified by age (AUC 0.920–0.980), sex (AUC 0.948–0.952), and glycemic control (AUC 0.938–0.954), and improved on high-quality images without media opacity (AUC 0.968–0.973) (Figure 2, p. 2219).
7. Despite clinically acceptable AUCs across all 10 external datasets, the DLS exhibited significantly lower specificity than human graders in the primary validation, indicating a higher false-positive rate that would increase downstream referral burden (Table 4, p. 2218).
17. Epistemic Classification
Classification: Foundational + Clinical Validation Precedent
Justification: This study is foundational for the field of multiethnic DR deep learning validation. Published in JAMA, it established the methodological standard for evaluating DLS across diverse populations and clinical settings. It serves as a clinical validation precedent by demonstrating feasibility within a real national screening program and proposing deployable screening models. It is not a benchmark study (uses private data) nor a Transformer-era study (CNN, 2017).
18. Analytical Synthesis
Ting et al. (2017) carries substantial epistemic weight as one of the earliest and most comprehensively validated deep learning studies for DR screening, published in a top-tier medical journal. Its primary contribution is demonstrating that a CNN-based DLS can achieve clinically acceptable diagnostic performance across 10 ethnically and geographically diverse external validation datasets, establishing a benchmark for cross-population generalization that few subsequent studies have matched in scope. For dissertation positioning, this study is a critical reference for arguments favoring rigorous external validation as the gold standard for evaluating DR DLS generalizability. The absence of preprocessing details in the main text is notable: the DLS achieves strong performance without any documented preprocessing pipeline, which could be interpreted as either irrelevance of preprocessing or simply underreporting. This ambiguity limits its utility for directly testing the preprocessing-dominance hypothesis, but it serves as an important counterpoint – suggesting that with sufficiently large and diverse training data, DLS performance may be robust without elaborate preprocessing. The study’s cross-dataset robustness (AUC 0.889–0.983) is among the strongest reported in the literature, though the consistently lower specificity compared to human graders flags a practical limitation for clinical deployment. Overall, this article strengthens the dissertation’s argument for mandatory external validation while remaining neutral on preprocessing effects.
— End of Literature Card —
